FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, Year 2 assessment (2018-2020), 4.0 release, 21 U.S. research sites, N=9,008.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original cross-sectional empirical analysis using linear regression to examine associations between SMAQ and alcohol expectancies (AEQ-AB).

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article in BMC Public Health; no indication of duplicate status.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Table 2 (Model 3) reports B=0.045 (95% CI 0.020-0.069) for SMAQ score predicting positive alcohol expectancies and B=0.072 (95% CI 0.043-0.101) for negative alcohol expectancies. SMAQ is a qualifying problematic SM scale; alcohol expectancies are individual-trait-level (substance-use belief outcome).

FT_IC3.evidence: Adjusted linear regression coefficients (B) with 95% CIs and p-values reported in Table 2 for SMAQ and social media time vs. alcohol expectancies.

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple quantitative estimates reported in Table 2 across three models for both positive and negative alcohol expectancy outcomes.

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design; EC7 requires longitudinal design as a threshold condition.
notes: SMAQ is the primary predictor (SM as IV); alcohol expectancies are the DV. Clear qualifying analysis with individual-trait outcome.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, Baseline and Year 2 (2018-2020), 4.0 release, 21 recruitment sites, N=9,673.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original prospective empirical analysis using generalized logistic and linear mixed effects models to examine associations between baseline ACE score and problematic screen use at Year 2.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article in BMC Public Health; no indication of duplicate status.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Table 2 reports OR for ACE score (individual-trait/trauma-exposure IV) predicting SMAQ-based problematic social media use and MPIQ-based problematic mobile phone use. ACEs are a qualifying individual-trait predictor; SMAQ and MPIQ are qualifying SM/phone variables. E.g., ACE 4+ vs. 0: OR=2.39 (95% CI 1.37-4.17) for problematic social media use (unadjusted); MPIQ OR=2.00 (95% CI 1.58-2.53).

FT_IC3.evidence: ORs with 95% CIs reported in Table 2 for both unadjusted and adjusted models for all three screen modalities (video game, social media, mobile phone).

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple quantitative OR estimates reported across ACE categories and screen modalities in Table 2 and Supplemental Tables.

FT_EC7_reason: All three EC7 conditions hold: (a) longitudinal prospective design (baseline ACEs predict Year 2 screen outcomes); (b) SM/phone variables (SMAQ, MPIQ, VGAQ) are DVs in every analysis; (c) no analysis with SM/phone as IV anywhere in the paper. No cross-sectional analysis with SM as DV is present to trigger the carve-out.
notes: ACEs qualify as individual-trait (trauma exposure) on the predictor side; SMAQ/MPIQ are SM/phone DVs. EC7 applies because the design is purely longitudinal with SM/phone as DV throughout and no SM-as-IV analysis anywhere.

FT_IC1.evidence: Explicitly discusses U.S. ABCD Study (abcdstudy.org), 21 U.S. data collection sites, national consortium.

FT_IC5.evidence: This is an editorial introduction to a special issue of Health Psychology. It describes and summarizes other empirical papers in the issue but contains no original empirical analysis of ABCD data.

notes: Special issue introduction/editorial; fails FT-IC5. Exclusion code FT-EC3.
